Okay, building your core tenants. So now that you have your client-centric mission, you understand what that is, I want you to understand the guiding principles behind it, okay? So this is how you intentionally build a culture rather than ending up with an accidental one. And what I want you to understand is there's a purpose behind the core tenants, and it will become more and more relevant as you grow, and you will see them become more and more important as you grow, okay? One, you use these as a soundboard or a set of principles to reflect the company culture. So I actually have mine always sitting next to me, and I look at them when I'm in interviews, I look at them when we're making decisions, I look at them on a daily basis to think, am I aligning with these right now? Okay, and it's really important that you have them written down. But they make the culture shaped more easily, and they also act as number two, which is they make decision-making easier, okay? So you think to yourself, do we need to launch this product or this product, or do I need to hire or fire this person, right? And you look at your core tenants, you're like, okay, does that show us going the extra mile? Does that seem like that person's being the one boss? Does that represent, grow or die? You know, whatever that may be, you're going to use those to filter your decision-making, right? Obviously, helping hire and fire. So every time I go through interviews, I think to myself, okay, how does this person rank at each of these core tenants, right? Because I want people who share similar values, okay? And then lastly, it's showing clients and potential teammates who you are, okay? So if your clients understand your core tenants, they're going to be able to also give you feedback on your culture, give you feedback on your decision-making, give you feedback on your business, as well as your teammates, okay? And so it's also, it's going to push away people who do not fit with your culture and attract those who do. The more you repeat these, the more you look by them, the easier it will get. And that is how you create a culture. It starts with these things, right? So in building your core tenants, let's talk about how to do it. And it is so simple to do this. There's no over-complication. You don't need to read a book on it. You don't need to go watch a YouTube video. Just freaking do what's written here. It's really easy in the sense of it just requires you to think and write a couple things down. It is not going to be incredibly time-consuming. It's just mentally stimulating. So one, who needs to be involved in deciding the core tenants, okay? So is it you? Is it you and two or three of your managers? Is it you and your spouse? Or is it your whole team? Who do you feel shapes your culture currently and has the most influence over it? Or if you currently have a much smaller team and it's just you and two people, you know, is this a good opportunity to bring those people in and allow them to have some ownership over the business, right? To brainstorm and write down your favorite qualities and traits about each member, teammate, et cetera, including yourself, and come up with a list of characteristics. And so Alex and I did this. It was, you know, almost over two years ago now. And we sat down and we thought, what is it that makes this person a great teammate? What is it that makes this person a great client? Okay. And we wrote down those qualities. After that, you want to ask yourself, okay, it doesn't have to be an alien, but if someone just came in from out of nowhere, right, and they want to explore your business, how would you describe the environment to them? Okay. So you're like, it's really, it's really fast place or it's a really slow paced place or it's really caring, loving, compassionate. It's a really like cutthroat. Is it, you know, what's the vibe when you walk into the building? What's the vibe in your core meetings? What's the vibe in your classes? Okay. Next, you want to change the verbiage of your values. So in order to avoid sounding boring as shit, to be honest, it needs to be incongruent with, like how you speak to your team and how you speak to your members. So are you funny? Are you smart? Are you hardworking? Are you straight to the point? Are you like hilarious and like, have humor? Do you have candor, like Alex or I? Think about how you communicate and make sure your core tends to learn with that. I think the most boring thing in the world you can do is just be like, be compassionate, be respectful, be loving. Like it doesn't have any personality behind it. So think of creative ways that you can say the same thing and it has a little bit more of your flavor to it. Okay. Present your values to the rest of the team. So how are you going to do this? You do not just hang up these things on your wall or decide like, hey guys, these are the values. Okay. You want to present the values, tell your team why you chose each one, and then open it up for questions and feedback. You need to make them feel like they are involved in this. Okay. And so that kind of transitions into distributing these. Okay. So one, you want to make sure that your core tenants are everywhere that is a mutually seen area. Right. So meaning it's going to be in all of your employee onboarding. It's going to be in your employee handbook. They're going to be hanging in the offices. They're going to be mentioned in all team trainings. You're going to mention them on every weekly and every monthly and every quarterly meeting. Okay. And then they're going to be used frequently when making decisions. So I keep mine obviously right next to myself. And when we are on meetings and we're making decisions, I'm constantly bumping back to those saying, okay, do I think that, yeah, but guys, like we said, don't sugarcoat it. So we should really just tell the clients what actually happened or like, yeah, well, I know you think this person like is trying really hard on all this, but I got it not sugarcoated and just tell you like the fricking suck. Right. So I keep that next to me all the time. So I make each repeat, repeat and live by these. Okay. And so this is an instance where you do not these core tenants, then you cannot ask others to live up to them. Okay. And so if you put, if you're very stern, you never celebrate, you never like to have fun. You're just like all about form and execution and profit and blah, blah, and you put something in there like be playful. They're going to be like, bro, you ain't playful. Right. And so you need to make sure that you are also, it's like when I said, you know, you have to live what you lead, you need to become these core tenants. If you feel that you want core tenants and you need to run your business by some that you currently do not embody, and that goes back to number one, which is you need to start becoming that person. Okay.
